subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: 2nd pass (2026-05-28, bias-corrected): Mexican independents (Animal Político, México Evalúa, Expansión) + IEP/Uppsala confirm the direction but FLAG the headline homicide drop as partly a disappearances/body-erasure artifact (integrated lethal violence ~-22%, not -44%); the 2027 judicial election was postponed to June 2028. Scores held; confidence in the violence improvement read down.

Live-data refresh (2026-05-28): factors re-validated against diverse, independent sources (multilateral bodies + non-US/non-Anglophone press; state-self-reported data still excluded). Notes mark what moved.
v2.0 source-discipline re-run applied. INEGI is generally credible but flagged partially-entangled given rising political pressure; confidence on its figures held moderate (not excluded).

Re-examined under skew discipline (prior outstanding item): nearshoring is scored as a real, partner-observable positive trajectory (trade long +3, economic positive skew) and the demographic dividend (median age ~29) is credited long-term. This lifts the asset/living reads relative to the earlier version.

Trade re-weighted 0.05 -> 0.17: near-term hostility from involuntary US tariff exposure, long-term nearshoring/diversification upside. Pemex oil dependence is a mild paradox-of-plenty fiscal drag, captured in fiscal_state.
Slant-balance audit: the cartel-violence country-average overstates personal risk in safe enclaves; institutional deterioration (elected judges) is independently sourced (WJP).
Scored on observable DATA over official reassurance; acute risk remains cartel violence.

economic.notes: Moderate debt, an autonomous central bank, and a genuine nearshoring tailwind, offset by the Pemex liability and US tariff exposure. Under the skew re-examination, the nearshoring/diversification trajectory is credited as a real long-term positive (trade long +3).

fiscal_state.notes: Deficit ~3.5% target under Sheinbaum; debt-to-GDP ~50% (moderate); Pemex a major liability and a mild paradox-of-plenty drag (oil dependence absorbing fiscal space).

monetary_independence.notes: Banxico cut to 6.50% (May 7, split vote) - autonomy intact (IMF, OECD, BBVA); inflation ~4.1% easing. Confidence up.

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: Peso not a reserve currency; neutral.

inflation.notes: Near the target band; peso weakness adds imported inflation.

banking_stability.notes: Stable, well-capitalized, foreign-bank dominated plus Banorte; competent CNBV.

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Mexican-source bias-correction (2026-05-28): record CONFIRMED via Expansion + Mexico Como Vamos - Q1 2026 FDI $23,591M, +10.4% YoY (>half now explicit nearshoring vs ~1/3 four years ago); electronics +58.7% (AI datacenters), autos +20.4%. BUT durability caveat strengthens, NOT weakens, near -3: USMCA review is a genuine renegotiation (3 rounds before 1-Jul; Canada excluded from first stage; Trump pushing tighter rules-of-origin to cut Chinese content + keeping auto/steel tariffs, per El Financiero/El Imparcial/Infobae). IMF/OECD see only 1.4-1.6% 2026 GDP with private investment 'constrained by uncertainty' - the FDI headline outruns the macro. Net: near -3 HELD, long +3 HELD; confidence 0.75.

institutional.notes: Major deterioration under the 2024 judicial reforms (popularly elected judges). Long-term composite worsens as the effects compound.

rule_of_law.notes: 2026 is the first full year of an elected judiciary; on 27-28 May 2026 Morena postponed the 2027 judicial election to June 2028 - a procedural reshuffle, not a reversal. Sharp drop in judge experience; new Judicial Discipline Tribunal can remove judges (Aristegui, La Jornada, CIDH; cross-checked beyond US think tanks). Held near -6/long -7.

statistical_integrity.notes: INEGI generally respected but pressures rising; flagged partially-entangled - figures used but cross-checked, confidence held moderate.

civil_service_capacity.notes: Hollowed during AMLO austerity; Pemex/CFE politicized; regulators (CRE, COFECE) weakened.

press_freedom.notes: Among the world's most dangerous countries for journalists; continued attacks on critical media.

political_social.notes: Cartel violence is the acute issue; welcoming treatment of expatriates and a favorable demographic profile are genuine offsets. Demographic dividend credited more fully under the skew re-examination.

political_stability.notes: Sheinbaum mandate, Morena supermajorities; stable but a weak opposition is concerning for institutional health.

civil_liberties.notes: Protections on paper but weak enforcement; surveillance issues; limited recourse.

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Visa rules unchanged (still net-welcoming to professionals/expats), but 2026 anti-gentrification protests in CDMX ('gringos go home') and rent-regulation plans turned sentiment, not policy. Nudged +3 -> +2.

social_violence.notes: IEP Mexico Peace Index confirms improvement (peace +5.1%, best since 2007), BUT Mexican independents (México Evalúa, Animal Político) show the ~44% headline is partly artifact - disappearances up ~35% since 2018 (>133k registry); integrated lethal violence falls only ~22%. Fear rose (75.6% feel unsafe, ENVIPE); Sinaloa homicides +70% from CJNG fragmentation. Held near -6/long -7; confidence trimmed to 0.75.

demographics.notes: Median age ~30; ~67% working-age; a genuine dividend, but CONAPO flags ~2030 as the turning point (over-60s overtake under-15s). Long credited at +2 (was +3) given the closing window.

geopolitical.notes: Few alliance dependencies to lose; no external wars; the internal cartel conflict is counterinsurgency-like.

alliance_reliability.notes: USMCA and the bilateral US relationship dominate; tariff/immigration/drug tensions; no fundamental alliance to lose; diversifying EU/LatAm.

conflict_involvement.notes: No external conflict; cartel violence internal; possible unilateral US strikes on cartels discussed, not implemented.

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No sanctions; free capital flows; limited control risk; some individual designations re: cartels.

physical_practical.notes: Excellent affordable private healthcare and a favorable highland climate offset by high violent crime and uneven infrastructure.

climate.notes: Northern/SW water stress plus dual-coast hurricanes (Otis 2023) plus heat, decent but strained readiness. (WB CCDR)

healthcare.notes: Public IMSS/ISSSTE inconsistent; private excellent and affordable; a medical-tourism destination.

infrastructure.notes: Major cities good; rural underdeveloped; grid reliability declined; water stressed.

crime_safety.notes: Among the highest violent crime globally; some safe enclaves (parts of CDMX, San Miguel, Queretaro, Merida).

disaster_insurance.notes: Earthquake and coastal hurricane exposure; insurance less developed but functional.

summary: A genuine nearshoring tailwind and a favourable demographic profile sit against serious institutional deterioration (the 2024 elected-judiciary reform) and acute cartel violence. The economic picture carries positive skew: record FDI and a diversification trajectory credited as durable upside. The headline homicide decline is real but partly a disappearances/erasure artifact, so confidence in the improvement is held moderate. Welcoming to foreign professionals on paper, though local sentiment toward newcomers has cooled.
